name: Out-of-date Version (WordPress)
description: It was identified that out-of-date version of Wordpress (Version detail) has been used.
remediation: Upgrade to the latest vendor-supported stable version and apply all current security patches. Maintain an inventory and a routine patch cadence (e.g., monthly) with emergency out-of-band updates for critical CVEs.

name: Out-of-date Version (Nginx)
description: It was identified that out-of-date version of Nginx (Version detail) has been used.
remediation: Upgrade to the latest vendor-supported stable version and apply all current security patches. Maintain an inventory and a routine patch cadence (e.g., monthly) with emergency out-of-band updates for critical CVEs.

name: Out-of-date Version (OpenSSL)
description: It was identified that out-of-date version of OpenSSL (Version detail) has been used.
remediation: Upgrade to the latest vendor-supported stable version and apply all current security patches. Maintain an inventory and a routine patch cadence (e.g., monthly) with emergency out-of-band updates for critical CVEs.

name: Out-of-date Version (Tomcat)
description: It was identified that out-of-date version of Tomcat (Version detail) has been used.
remediation: Upgrade to the latest vendor-supported stable version and apply all current security patches. Maintain an inventory and a routine patch cadence (e.g., monthly) with emergency out-of-band updates for critical CVEs.

name: Out-of-date Version (Bootstrap)
description: It was identified that out-of-date version of Bootstrap (Version detail) has been used.
remediation: Upgrade to the latest vendor-supported stable version and apply all current security patches. Maintain an inventory and a routine patch cadence (e.g., monthly) with emergency out-of-band updates for critical CVEs.

name: Out-of-date Version (jQuery)
description: It was identified that out-of-date version of jQuery (Version detail) has been used.
remediation: Upgrade to the latest vendor-supported stable version and apply all current security patches. Maintain an inventory and a routine patch cadence (e.g., monthly) with emergency out-of-band updates for critical CVEs.

name: Out-of-date Version
description: It was identified that out-of-date version of a component (Version detail) has been used.
remediation: Upgrade to the latest vendor-supported stable version and apply all current security patches. Maintain an inventory and a routine patch cadence (e.g., monthly) with emergency out-of-band updates for critical CVEs.

name: Out-of-date Version (Apache)
description: It was identified that out-of-date version of Apache (Version detail) has been used.
remediation: Upgrade to the latest vendor-supported stable version and apply all current security patches. Maintain an inventory and a routine patch cadence (e.g., monthly) with emergency out-of-band updates for critical CVEs.

name: CRLF Injection
description: CRLF injection vulnerability exists if an attacker can inject the CRLF characters into a web application, for example using a user input form or an HTTP request.
remediation: Sanitize and reject CR and LF characters in untrusted input used in headers; use frameworks that auto-encode header values.

name: Server Side Request Forgery
description: A Server Side Request Forgery (SSRF) attack can often result in unauthorized actions or access to data within the organization, either in the vulnerable application itself or on other back-end systems that the application can communicate with. In some situations, the SSRF vulnerability might allow an attacker to perform arbitrary command execution.
remediation: Block outbound traffic by default, use network-layer egress controls, URL allowlists, and metadata service protections (IMDSv2 / hop-limits).

name: Default Credentials Used
description: A default password is a password (usually '123', 'admin', 'root', 'password', 'secret', or 'access') assigned to a program or hardware device by the developer or manufacturer. Although default passwords may help protect from some users, anyone who knows what they are doing can usually guess a default password.
remediation: Eliminate default credentials, enforce strong unique secrets, rotate regularly, and add MFA.

name: Unauthorized Access
description: Access to other user data or manipulation of the other user using POST data parameter tampering.
remediation: Centralize authorization checks, enforce RBAC/ABAC, validate server-side, and deny-by-default for sensitive endpoints.

name: Privilege Escalation
description: Any user can gain admin access by accessing admin URL or manipulating the request/response.
remediation: Apply secure defaults: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.

name: Parameter Manipulation
description: Attacker can manipulate parameters to access any user data and it can lead to further attacks.
remediation: Apply secure defaults: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.

name: Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR)
description: Attacker, who is an authorized system user, simply changes a parameter value that directly refers to a system object to another object the user is not authorized for. Such flaws can compromise all the data that can be referenced by the parameter.
remediation: Enforce object-level authorization checks on every request, use indirect references, and validate ownership on the server side.

name: Security Misconfiguration
description: Attackers will often attempt to exploit risky open ports to gain unauthorized access or knowledge of the system.
remediation: Close unnecessary ports, apply principle of least privilege to all services, disable unused features, and conduct regular configuration audits.

name: Brute Force Attack
description: Brute force attacks are simple and reliable. Attackers let a computer do the work, trying different combinations of usernames and passwords.
remediation: Implement rate limiting, CAPTCHAs, password policies (length/complexity), account lockout with monitored thresholds, and login throttling. Prefer passwordless/MFA where feasible.

name: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
description: Cross-site scripting vulnerabilities arise when user input is stored and later embedded into the application responses in an unsafe way. An attacker can use the vulnerability to inject malicious JavaScript code into the application, which will execute within the browser of any user who views the relevant application content.
remediation: Enforce contextual output encoding, validate and sanitize inputs, deploy a strong CSP (default-src 'self'), and use frameworks that auto-escape. Avoid using dangerous sinks or sanitize with vetted libraries (e.g., DOMPurify for client-side).

name: HTML Injection
description: When an application does not properly handle user supplied data, an attacker can supply valid HTML code, typically via a parameter value, and inject their own content into the page. This is similar to XSS but limited to HTML tag injection.
remediation: Enforce contextual output encoding, validate and sanitize inputs, deploy a strong CSP (default-src 'self'), and use frameworks that auto-escape.

name: Host Header Injection
description: Developers are trusting the HTTP Host header value and using it to generate links, import scripts and even generate password reset links with its value. This is a bad idea, because the HTTP Host header can be controlled by an attacker and exploited using web-cache poisoning or by abusing password reset emails.
remediation: Do not trust the Host header. Validate against a server-side allowlist and use server_name. Configure a default vhost that rejects unknown hosts.

name: Insecure Data Transmission
description: Information transmitted over an unencrypted channel. This information should always be transferred via an encrypted channel (HTTPS) to avoid being intercepted by malicious users.
remediation: Enforce HTTPS everywhere with HSTS (max-age >= 31536000; includeSubDomains; preload), disable weak protocols/ciphers, and use modern TLS configuration with valid certificates.

name: Blind SQL Injection
description: It is possible to gain access to the database by injecting SQL commands into data-plane input in order to affect the execution of predefined SQL commands.
remediation: Use parameterized queries / prepared statements, enforce server-side input validation, limit DB privileges (least privilege), and implement centralized query builders. Add WAF rules for known injection patterns.

name: Unvalidated URL Redirect
description: It is possible to redirect a user to an untrusted site when the user visits a link located on a trusted website.
remediation: Avoid open redirects. Use internal IDs mapped to destinations and validate redirect targets against an allowlist.

name: Authentication Bypass
description: It was identified that all users have the same privilege with admin rights, allowing authentication to be bypassed.
remediation: Apply secure defaults: enforce strong authentication, validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.

name: Authorization Bypass
description: It was identified that all users have the same privilege with admin rights, allowing authorization to be bypassed.
remediation: Centralize authorization checks, enforce RBAC/ABAC, validate server-side, and deny-by-default for sensitive endpoints.

name: OTP Bypass
description: OTP Verification can be bypassed using Virtual Free Numbers. There are various apps/tools which provide Virtual Free Numbers using which OTP verification can be bypassed.
remediation: Apply secure defaults: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.

name: Weak Session Management
description: Session management governs interactions between a web-based application and users. Weak session management can allow session hijacking, enabling attackers to impersonate legitimate users.
remediation: Rotate session IDs after login, set HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite cookie flags, short idle/absolute timeouts, invalidate on logout, and store minimal server-side session state.

name: Session Fixation
description: The application does not renew its session cookie(s) after a successful user authentication, enabling session hijacking and infiltration attacks.
remediation: Rotate session IDs after login, set HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite cookie flags, short idle/absolute timeouts, invalidate on logout, and store minimal server-side session state.

name: SQL Query Disclosure
description: This page contains one or more comments that look like SQL statements. These SQL statements may disclose sensitive information to an attacker.
remediation: Apply secure defaults: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.

name: Email Addresses Disclosed
description: Spam bots known as email harvesters scour the internet looking for email addresses on websites. They look for strings like name@domain.com and record any addresses found, which can lead to spam and phishing attacks.
remediation: Remove sensitive artifacts, restrict access, and return generic error pages; log server-side securely.

name: Database Connection String Disclosed
description: A database connection string specifies information about a data source and the means of connecting to it. Exposing this in web applications is a critical security risk.
remediation: Apply secure defaults: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.

name: Auto-Complete Feature Enabled
description: An attacker with local access could obtain the cleartext password from the browser cache, if the username is stored as part of autocomplete functionality.
remediation: Disable autocomplete on sensitive form fields (passwords, credit card numbers) using autocomplete='off'.

name: ASP.NET Debugging Enabled
description: ASP.NET debugging is enabled on this application. Debug mode displays a detailed traceback including metadata about the environment, such as all the currently defined settings, which may disclose sensitive information.
remediation: Disable verbose headers and debug pages in production, remove stack traces and comments, and expose only minimal, necessary information.

name: Insecure Frame (External)
description: IFrame sandboxing enables a set of additional restrictions for the content within a frame to restrict potentially malicious code from causing harm to the web page that embeds it.
remediation: Apply secure defaults: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.

name: Microsoft IIS Tilde Directory Enumeration
description: It is possible to detect short names of files and directories which have an 8.3 file naming scheme equivalent in Windows by using some vectors in several versions of Microsoft IIS.
remediation: Upgrade to the latest vendor-supported stable version and apply all current security patches. Maintain an inventory and a routine patch cadence.

name: Default Server Page Disclosure
description: It was identified that an IP with port 8080 is redirected to Web Server Sample document pages, disclosing server information.
remediation: Remove or restrict access to default server pages and sample documents in production environments.

name: Microsoft IIS Server Config File Found
description: Microsoft Internet Information Services (IIS) installs a file named service.cnf. This file may contain sensitive information, which could allow a remote attacker to launch further attacks.
remediation: Apply secure defaults: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.

name: Reverse Tabnabbing
description: Open windows with hrefs using the tag target='_blank' can modify window.opener.location and replace the parent webpage with something else, even on a different origin.
remediation: Add rel='noopener noreferrer' to all anchor tags with target='_blank' to prevent the opened page from accessing the opener's context.

name: X-Powered-By Header Disclosure
description: Revealing server information (x-powered-by: Asp.net) might enable the attacker to exploit vulnerabilities associated with the corresponding application type.
remediation: Disable verbose headers in production: remove X-Powered-By, Server, and X-AspNet-Version headers from all responses.

name: Test Page Detected
description: Testing-related application pages were found. Test pages are usually implemented ad-hoc and often do not adhere to the security requirements of the rest of the application, making them a potential security hazard.
remediation: Remove all test pages, debug endpoints, and sample content from production environments before deployment.

name: Java Servlet Version Disclosure in HTTP Headers
description: The HTTP responses returned by this web application include a header naming the Java Servlet and JSP version.
remediation: Configure the application server to suppress version information from HTTP response headers.

name: X-AspNet-Version Header Disclosure
description: The value of this header discloses the version of ASP.NET in use. It is not necessary for production sites and should be disabled.
remediation: Disable verbose headers in production: remove X-Powered-By, Server, and X-AspNet-Version headers from all responses.

name: X-AspNetMvc-Version Header Disclosure
description: The value of this header discloses the version of ASP.NET MVC in use. It is not necessary for production sites and should be disabled.
remediation: Disable verbose headers in production: remove X-Powered-By, Server, and X-AspNetMvc-Version headers from all responses.

name: MicrosoftSharePointTeamServices Header Disclosure
description: The value of this header discloses the version of MicrosoftSharePointTeamServices in use. It is not necessary for production sites and should be disabled.
remediation: Upgrade to the latest vendor-supported stable version, apply all security patches, and suppress version disclosure headers.

name: Server Banner Disclosure
description: The Webserver details are disclosed which will help the attackers to narrow down the scope of attack. It is advisable not to disclose such information.
remediation: Disable verbose headers and debug pages in production, remove stack traces and comments, and expose only minimal, necessary information.

name: Directory Listing
description: The web server is configured to display the list of files contained in a directory. This is not recommended because the directory may contain files not normally exposed through links, potentially exposing sensitive information.
remediation: Disable directory listing/indexing on the web server and restrict access to sensitive paths.

name: Weak Lockout Mechanism
description: Account lockout mechanisms are used to mitigate brute force password guessing attacks. Accounts are typically locked after 3 to 5 unsuccessful login attempts and can only be unlocked after a predetermined period of time.
remediation: Implement rate limiting, CAPTCHAs, password policies (length/complexity), account lockout with monitored thresholds, and login throttling. Prefer passwordless/MFA where feasible.

name: Insecure TLS Version Enabled
description: An attacker may be able to exploit this problem to conduct man-in-the-middle attacks and decrypt communications between the affected service and clients.
remediation: Disable TLS 1.0 and TLS 1.1. Enforce TLS 1.2 or TLS 1.3 with strong cipher suites. Use HSTS to enforce HTTPS.

name: Remove Weak Ciphers in TLS 1.2
description: An attacker may be able to exploit weak cipher configurations to conduct man-in-the-middle attacks and decrypt communications between the affected service and clients.
remediation: Enforce HTTPS everywhere with HSTS, disable weak protocols/ciphers (RC4, DES, 3DES, NULL), and use modern TLS configuration with valid certificates.

name: Invalid SSL Certificate
description: An invalid SSL certificate makes it difficult for visitors to distinguish between your certificate and those of attackers, potentially indicating an attempt to fool or intercept data.
remediation: Obtain and install a valid SSL/TLS certificate from a trusted Certificate Authority (CA). Implement HSTS and ensure certificates are renewed before expiry.

name: Session Token in URL
description: Application is forwarding Session token in URL. Sensitive information within URLs may be logged in various locations, including the browser, the web server, and any proxy servers between the two endpoints.
remediation: Rotate session IDs after login, set HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite cookie flags, short idle/absolute timeouts, invalidate on logout, and store minimal server-side session state.

name: XML Entity Expansion
description: An attacker submitting XML document to the target application using nested entity expansion to produce an excessively large output can create excessive demands on a processor's CPU and memory (Billion Laughs attack).
remediation: Disable DTDs/external entities, use secure XML parsers, and validate input schemas. Implement size limits on XML input.

name: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) Misconfiguration
description: If a website responds with Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * the requested resource allows sharing with every origin. Therefore, any website can make XHR requests to your site and access the responses.
remediation: Set Access-Control-Allow-Origin to an allowlist of trusted origins, avoid wildcard with credentials, and validate preflight responses. Disable reflection of the Origin header.

name: Cross Site Request Forgery (CSRF)
description: Cross-site request forgery is a type of malicious exploit of a website whereby unauthorized commands are transmitted from a user that the website trusts.
remediation: Use synchronizer/double-submit CSRF tokens tied to user sessions, SameSite=Strict cookies, and require re-authentication for sensitive actions.

name: Directory Indexing
description: Depending on the operating system and distribution, the default configuration of Apache allows the indexing of directories, enabling search engines to index the file structure and possibly revealing sensitive data.
remediation: Disable directory listing/indexing on the web server and restrict access to sensitive paths.

name: Insecure HTTP Methods Enabled
description: HTTP OPTIONS method is enabled on this web server. The OPTIONS method provides a list of methods that are supported by the web server, which can assist attackers in understanding the attack surface.
remediation: Disable unused HTTP methods (OPTIONS, TRACE, DELETE, PUT) at the web server level. Allow only GET and POST for standard web applications.

name: Missing Strict-Transport-Security Header
description: HTTP strict transport security (HSTS) enforces connections in HTTPS disregarding any script's call to load any resource in that domain over HTTP.
remediation: Enforce HTTPS everywhere with HSTS (max-age >= 31536000; includeSubDomains; preload), disable weak protocols/ciphers, and use modern TLS configuration with valid certificates.

name: Change Password Feature Missing
description: In the application there is no change password feature and any user can change passwords for any users without admin rights.
remediation: Implement a secure change password feature that requires knowledge of the current password, validates complexity requirements, and enforces re-authentication.

name: Sensitive Information in URL
description: Information exposure through query strings in URL is when sensitive data is passed to parameters in the URL. This allows attackers to obtain sensitive data such as usernames, passwords, tokens, database details. Simply using HTTPS does not resolve this vulnerability.
remediation: Move sensitive parameters from URL query strings to POST request bodies or HTTP headers. Enforce HTTPS with HSTS.

name: Unprotected File
description: Insecure deployment and exposure of files can supply attackers with invaluable information about the application enabling them to orchestrate targeted attacks.
remediation: Apply secure defaults: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.

name: Sensitive Information Disclosure
description: It is possible to view server documentation, server information and server sample pages in the browser, which may reveal sensitive configuration details.
remediation: Disable verbose headers and debug pages in production, remove stack traces and comments, and expose only minimal, necessary information.

name: CSV Injection
description: It occurs when data in the file is not properly validated prior to export. The attacker injects a malicious payload or formula into the input field. Once exported and opened, the spreadsheet application executes the malicious payload.
remediation: Prefix cell values that start with =, +, -, or @ with a single quote. Validate and sanitize all data before export.

name: Weak Encoding
description: It was identified that Base64 encoding is used to encode sensitive data. Encoding is not encryption and provides no security for sensitive information.
remediation: Use proper encryption algorithms (AES-256) for sensitive data at rest and in transit. Do not use encoding as a substitute for encryption.

name: Anti-Automation
description: It was identified that an attacker can perform form-based brute force or dictionary attack to compromise the application due to missing anti-automation controls.
remediation: Implement rate limiting, CAPTCHAs, password policies (length/complexity), account lockout with monitored thresholds, and login throttling. Prefer passwordless/MFA where feasible.

name: Complex Password Policy Missing
description: It was identified that the application does not follow the password policy and accepts guessable words (dictionary words).
remediation: Implement a strong password policy requiring minimum length (12+ chars), complexity (upper/lower/numbers/symbols), and reject common dictionary words. Consider using password strength meters.

name: Sensitive Information Disclosure in Cookie
description: It was identified that user information and IP address are stored in the Cookie, exposing sensitive data to potential interception.
remediation: Disable verbose headers and debug pages in production. Set HttpOnly, Secure, and SameSite cookie flags. Avoid storing sensitive information in cookies.

name: Concurrent Login Enabled
description: It was identified that the same session is being used by multiple users at the same time (Multi Login enabled), which can lead to unauthorized access.
remediation: Rotate session IDs after login, set HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite cookie flags, short idle/absolute timeouts, invalidate on logout, and enforce single-session-per-user policy.

name: Weak Password Change/Reset Functionality
description: It was noticed that while changing the Password it does not ask for Old Password, so any user can change password without an old password.
remediation: Implement rate limiting, CAPTCHAs, password policies (length/complexity), account lockout with monitored thresholds, and require current password verification for password changes.

name: CCS Injection (SSL/TLS MITM Vulnerability)
description: OpenSSL's ChangeCipherSpec processing has a serious vulnerability. This vulnerability allows malicious intermediate nodes to intercept encrypted data and decrypt them while forcing SSL clients to use weak keys.
remediation: Enforce HTTPS everywhere with HSTS, disable weak protocols/ciphers, upgrade OpenSSL to the latest stable version, and use modern TLS configuration with valid certificates.

name: Improper Error Handling
description: Potential vulnerability due to the presence of a fully qualified path name to the root of the system in error messages. This information helps the attacker to understand about the application to enumerate further.
remediation: Configure custom error pages for all error codes. Return generic error messages to users and log detailed errors server-side only.

name: Unrestricted URL Access
description: Sensitive application URLs can be accessed directly without proper authentication or authorization checks, allowing unauthorized users to access restricted functionality.
remediation: Apply secure defaults: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.

name: Missing Secure Cookie Attributes
description: Secure and http-only attributes need to be added to cookie flags of all pages to prevent cookie theft via JavaScript or network interception.
remediation: Set Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite attributes on all cookies; avoid sensitive data in cookies; encrypt/sign values and minimize lifetime.

name: Encode Used Instead of Encryption
description: Sensitive information about passwords are encoded (e.g., Base64) and stored. Encoding is reversible by anyone and provides no security.
remediation: Use strong encryption algorithms (AES-256-GCM) for sensitive data at rest. Use bcrypt/Argon2/PBKDF2 for password hashing. Never use encoding as a substitute for encryption.

name: Missing X-Content-Type-Options
description: Setting this header will prevent the browser from MIME-sniffing a response away from the declared content-type, preventing MIME type confusion attacks.
remediation: Add X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff header to all HTTP responses.

name: Session Not Terminated After Logout
description: The application does not invalidate current session cookie(s) after a user logout. An expired cookie can be used to authenticate a malicious user.
remediation: Rotate session IDs after login, set HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite cookie flags, short idle/absolute timeouts, invalidate on logout, and store minimal server-side session state.

name: Missing X-Frame-Options Header
description: The web server did not return an X-Frame-Options header which means that this website could be at risk of a clickjacking attack.
remediation: Send X-Frame-Options: DENY (or SAMEORIGIN as needed) and a restrictive Content-Security-Policy frame-ancestors directive.

name: Missing Content Security Policy Header
description: This response header allows web site administrators to control resources the user agent is allowed to load for a given page. This helps guard against cross-site scripting attacks (XSS).
remediation: Enforce contextual output encoding, validate and sanitize inputs, deploy a strong CSP (default-src 'self'), and use frameworks that auto-escape.

name: Filepath Disclosure
description: Bad input can lead to an internal application error which reveals the path information, exploiting the application business logic to reveal path disclosures.
remediation: Remove sensitive artifacts, restrict access, and return generic error pages; log server-side securely.

name: Unencrypted View State Parameter
description: To reduce the chance of someone intercepting the information stored in the ViewState, it has to be encrypted.
remediation: Enable ViewState encryption and MAC validation in ASP.NET applications. Set enableViewStateMAC='true' and ViewStateEncryptionMode='Always'.

name: Browser Cache Weakness
description: Unless directed otherwise, browsers may store a local cached copy of content received from web servers. If sensitive information is stored in the local cache, it may be retrieved by other users who have access to the same computer.
remediation: Set Cache-Control: no-store, no-cache headers for sensitive pages. Use Pragma: no-cache for older browser compatibility.

name: User Enumeration
description: User enumeration is when a malicious actor can use brute-force techniques to either guess or confirm valid users in a system. This can be exploited to build a list of valid usernames for further attacks.
remediation: Return identical error messages for failed login attempts regardless of whether the username exists. Implement rate limiting and CAPTCHA on login forms.

name: Source Code Disclosure
description: Using a source code disclosure attack, an attacker can retrieve the source code of server-side scripts such as ASP, PHP and JSP. This grants the attacker deeper knowledge of the application logic, database structure, and vulnerabilities.
remediation: Remove sensitive artifacts, restrict access to source files, and return generic error pages; log server-side securely.

name: Unrestricted File Upload
description: Various web applications allow users to upload files. If not handled correctly, a remote attacker could upload a file of a dangerous type that can be automatically processed within the product environment, leading to remote code execution.
remediation: Use a strict allowlist of file types, verify MIME and content, store outside webroot, randomize filenames, and scan files. Block executable handling and enforce size limits.

name: Using Components with Known Vulnerabilities
description: Vulnerable versions of third-party components (e.g., jQuery, Bootstrap) have been used in the application, which may expose known security vulnerabilities.
remediation: Upgrade to the latest vendor-supported stable version and apply all current security patches. Maintain an inventory and a routine patch cadence with emergency out-of-band updates for critical CVEs.

name: HTTP Verb Tampering
description: GET and POST methods may be used interchangeably. An attacker can switch HTTP methods to bypass access controls or perform unintended state-changing operations.
remediation: Enforce consistent HTTP method usage. Validate that state-changing operations only accept POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE. Apply access controls to all HTTP methods equally.

name: Missing XSS Protection Header
description: X-XSS-protection header helps in enabling the cross-site scripting (XSS) filter built into browsers.
remediation: Enforce contextual output encoding, validate and sanitize inputs, deploy a strong CSP (default-src 'self'), and add X-XSS-Protection: 1; mode=block header.

name: DEBUG Enabled
description: Django web application is running with debug mode turned on (DEBUG = True). This displays a detailed traceback including a lot of metadata about the environment when an exception occurs.
remediation: Disable verbose headers and debug pages in production: set DEBUG=False, configure custom error handlers, and never expose stack traces to end users.

name: Silverlight Cross-Domain Policy
description: The application publishes a Silverlight cross-domain policy which allows access from any domain. Any domain can perform two-way interaction with this application, presenting a significant security risk.
remediation: Apply secure defaults: restrict cross-domain policies to specific trusted domains and validate all cross-origin requests.

name: Implement Session Timeout Policy
description: Session timeout represents the event occurring when a user does not perform any action on a web site during a defined interval. Without proper timeout, sessions remain active indefinitely.
remediation: Rotate session IDs after login, set HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite cookie flags, short idle/absolute timeouts (15-30 minutes), invalidate on logout, and store minimal server-side session state.

name: Content Spoofing
description: Content spoofing is an attack targeting a user made possible by an injection vulnerability in a web application. An attacker can supply content to a web application that is reflected back to the user under the context of the trusted domain.
remediation: Apply secure defaults: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.

name: No Vulnerability Found
description: No active vulnerabilities have been identified during this assessment period.
remediation: Continue to maintain secure coding practices, regular security assessments, and routine patch cadence.

name: Out-of-date Version (IIS)
description: It was identified that out-of-date version of IIS (Version detail) has been used.
remediation: Upgrade to the latest vendor-supported stable version and apply all current security patches. Maintain an inventory and a routine patch cadence.

name: Out-of-date Version (jQuery UI Dialog)
description: It was identified that out-of-date version of jQuery UI Dialog (Version detail) has been used.
remediation: Upgrade to the latest vendor-supported stable version and apply all current security patches. Maintain an inventory and a routine patch cadence.

name: Out-of-date Version (jQuery UI Autocomplete)
description: It was identified that out-of-date version of jQuery UI Autocomplete (Version detail) has been used.
remediation: Upgrade to the latest vendor-supported stable version and apply all current security patches. Maintain an inventory and a routine patch cadence.

name: Out-of-date Version (jQuery UI Tooltip)
description: It was identified that out-of-date version of jQuery UI Tooltip (Version detail) has been used.
remediation: Upgrade to the latest vendor-supported stable version and apply all current security patches. Maintain an inventory and a routine patch cadence.

name: Out-of-date Version (Moment.js)
description: It was identified that out-of-date version of Moment.js (Version detail) has been used.
remediation: Upgrade to the latest vendor-supported stable version and apply all current security patches. Maintain an inventory and a routine patch cadence.

name: Out-of-date Version (Modernizr)
description: It was identified that out-of-date version of Modernizr (Version detail) has been used.
remediation: Upgrade to the latest vendor-supported stable version and apply all current security patches. Maintain an inventory and a routine patch cadence.

name: Out-of-date Version (Highcharts)
description: It was identified that out-of-date version of Highcharts (Version detail) has been used.
remediation: Upgrade to the latest vendor-supported stable version and apply all current security patches. Maintain an inventory and a routine patch cadence.

name: Out-of-date Version (D3.js)
description: It was identified that out-of-date version of D3.js (Version detail) has been used.
remediation: Upgrade to the latest vendor-supported stable version and apply all current security patches. Maintain an inventory and a routine patch cadence.

name: BREACH Attack Detected
description: Weak nonce value is used in the HTML attributes, making the application susceptible to the BREACH compression attack.
remediation: Apply secure defaults: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.

name: Session Hijacking
description: Session Hijacking attack compromises the session token by stealing or predicting a valid session token to gain unauthorized access to the Web Server.
remediation: Rotate session IDs after login, set HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite cookie flags, short idle/absolute timeouts, invalidate on logout, and store minimal server-side session state.

name: Duplicate Headers
description: Duplicate headers are present in response headers, which can lead to inconsistent interpretation by clients and security controls.
remediation: Configure the web server to send each security header exactly once. Audit all middleware and framework configurations that add response headers.

name: CSP-Unsafe Directives Used
description: It was identified that unsafe-inline and unsafe-eval directives are used in the Content Security Policy header.
remediation: Define a strict CSP (no unsafe-inline/eval), use nonces/hashes for scripts, and limit sources to trusted origins.

name: Unvalidated Input
description: It was identified that special characters and malicious JavaScript payloads are accepted in the input field with no restrictions.
remediation: Apply secure defaults: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.

name: Sensitive Data in Local Storage
description: An attacker can gather sensitive information based on the data stored locally in the browser's local storage.
remediation: Do not store sensitive data (tokens, passwords, PII) in localStorage or sessionStorage. Use secure, HttpOnly cookies for authentication tokens.

name: Missing Character Length Validation
description: An attacker may flood the input field with a huge amount of characters, potentially crashing the server.
remediation: Implement server-side input length validation and enforce maximum character limits on all input fields.

name: Broken Functionality
description: Part of the application was not functional during the testing period.
remediation: Review application functionality and fix broken features before production deployment. Implement automated testing to detect functional regressions.

name: Broken Access Control
description: Broken access control is a type of vulnerability that allows unauthorized users to gain access to sensitive data or systems when controls such as authentication and authorization are not properly implemented.
remediation: Centralize authorization checks, enforce RBAC/ABAC, validate server-side, and deny-by-default for sensitive endpoints.

name: Parameter Tampering
description: Attacker can manipulate parameters to access any user data, leading to further attacks.
remediation: Centralize authorization checks, enforce RBAC/ABAC, validate server-side, and deny-by-default for sensitive endpoints.

name: Missing Character Limit Validation
description: It has been identified that the character limit has not been set in the input field.
remediation: Apply secure defaults: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.

name: No Character Set Specified
description: Not specifying a character set increases the risk of client-side attacks, such as cross-site scripting.
remediation: Enforce contextual output encoding, validate and sanitize inputs, deploy a strong CSP (default-src 'self'), and use frameworks that auto-escape.

name: CSP Header Misconfiguration
description: It has been found that unsafe directives are used in the Content Security Policy Header which can allow the execution of unsafe scripts.
remediation: Define a strict CSP (no unsafe-inline/eval), use nonces/hashes for scripts, and limit sources to trusted origins.

name: Unrestricted Resource Consumption
description: It has been identified that the API rate limit for the process has not been set, allowing potential abuse.
remediation: Implement API rate limiting, request throttling, and resource quotas. Monitor and alert on unusual consumption patterns.

name: Business Logic Error
description: A malicious user may modify system data in a way that disrupts the business logic or processes.
remediation: Apply secure defaults: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.

name: Broken Object Level Authorization
description: Unauthorized access to other users' objects can result in data disclosure, data loss, or data manipulation. Under certain circumstances, this can also lead to full account takeover.
remediation: Enforce object-level authorization checks on every request, use indirect references, and validate ownership on the server side.

name: Insufficient Session Expiration
description: Insufficient session expiration allows attackers to reuse old session credentials or IDs, potentially gaining unauthorized access and escalating security risks.
remediation: Rotate session IDs after login, set HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite cookie flags, short idle/absolute timeouts, invalidate on logout, and store minimal server-side session state.

name: Mass Assignment
description: Mass assignment occurs when an API automatically binds client-provided data to internal objects without proper filtering. This can allow attackers to modify sensitive properties they should not have access to, leading to potential security breaches.
remediation: Apply secure defaults: validate and sanitize inputs, enforce least privilege, enable security headers, keep components patched, and add monitoring/alerting for anomalies.
